ingredients.name: Eicosapentaenoic Acid
ingredients.notes: EPA form not specified. Conservative scoring. EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) is the primary anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acid. Form affects bioavailability — check product label.

condition_hits.mechanism: EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) competitively inhibits arachidonic acid conversion to thromboxane A2 via cyclooxygenase, and generates thromboxane A3 (a much weaker platelet aggregator) instead. This antiplatelet mechanism prolongs bleeding time, particularly at doses above 2 g/day.
condition_hits.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit EPA intake to under 2 g/day without physician oversight. Disclose use to any treating surgeon.
condition_hits.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding EPA.
condition_hits.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: EPA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may affect the vitamin K-dependent coagulation cascade. Clinical studies with high-dose fish oil (containing EPA) and warfarin show small but consistent INR elevations.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor INR when starting or stopping high-dose EPA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin or other anticoagulants.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: EPA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect: EPA reduces TXA2 while clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor, producing complementary inhibition of platelet aggregation pathways. Combined bleeding risk is greater than with either agent alone.
drug_class_hits.action: Inform prescriber of EPA supplement use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid doses >3 g/day without haematological oversight.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

ingredients.name: Docosahexaenoic Acid
ingredients.notes: DHA form not specified. Conservative scoring. DHA (docosahexaenoic acid, 22:6 n-3) is critical for brain, retina, and cardiovascular health. Form significantly affects absorption — check product label.

condition_hits.mechanism: DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) is incorporated into platelet phospholipid membranes, displacing arachidonic acid and reducing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis. TXA2 is the primary platelet aggregation amplifier; reduced TXA2 production measurably prolongs bleeding time at doses above 2 g/day.
condition_hits.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit DHA to doses under 2 g/day unless under physician oversight. Inform surgeon of use before any procedure.
condition_hits.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding DHA.
condition_hits.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: DHA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may weakly inhibit vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor synthesis. Combined with warfarin, a small but clinically relevant INR elevation has been observed in controlled studies.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor INR when adding or removing high-dose DHA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to use.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: DHA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect via independent mechanisms: clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor while DHA reduces TXA2 production. The combination further impairs platelet aggregation beyond either agent alone.
drug_class_hits.action: Advise prescriber of DHA use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid high-dose supplements (>3 g/day) without haematology guidance.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

inactive_ingredients.name: Gelatin
inactive_ingredients.notes: Derived from animal collagen, forming the shell of traditional capsules and softgels. Provides a protective casing. While animal-derived (not vegan/vegetarian), it is generally considered safe and inert in its role as a capsule material.

inactive_ingredients.name: Glycerin
inactive_ingredients.notes: Plant-derived glycerol (1,2,3-propanetriol, E422) used as humectant, solvent, and sweetener in supplement formulations. FDA GRAS. Essential for gummy texture and shelf life. Acts as carrier for flavors and herbal extracts. ADI: not specified (JECFA — acceptable for food use).

inactive_ingredients.name: natural Lemon flavor
inactive_ingredients.notes: Flavoring substances derived from plant or animal sources. While generally recognized as safe, the term can encompass hundreds of compounds. Must be derived from natural sources per FDA regulations. Good clean label choice.

inactive_ingredients.name: Vitamin E
inactive_ingredients.notes: Antioxidants used to prevent spoilage, such as fat-soluble Vitamin C (ascorbyl palmitate) or Vitamin E (tocopherols). Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) and beneficial. Premium clean label choice.

warnings.title: Eicosapentaenoic Acid / bleeding_disorders
warnings.detail: EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) competitively inhibits arachidonic acid conversion to thromboxane A2 via cyclooxygenase, and generates thromboxane A3 (a much weaker platelet aggregator) instead. This antiplatelet mechanism prolongs bleeding time, particularly at doses above 2 g/day.
warnings.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit EPA intake to under 2 g/day without physician oversight. Disclose use to any treating surgeon.
warnings.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding EPA.
warnings.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

warnings.detail: EPA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may affect the vitamin K-dependent coagulation cascade. Clinical studies with high-dose fish oil (containing EPA) and warfarin show small but consistent INR elevations.
warnings.action: Monitor INR when starting or stopping high-dose EPA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin or other anticoagulants.
warnings.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: EPA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.detail: Additive antiplatelet effect: EPA reduces TXA2 while clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor, producing complementary inhibition of platelet aggregation pathways. Combined bleeding risk is greater than with either agent alone.
warnings.action: Inform prescriber of EPA supplement use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid doses >3 g/day without haematological oversight.
warnings.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA.
warnings.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

warnings.title: Docosahexaenoic Acid / bleeding_disorders
warnings.detail: DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) is incorporated into platelet phospholipid membranes, displacing arachidonic acid and reducing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis. TXA2 is the primary platelet aggregation amplifier; reduced TXA2 production measurably prolongs bleeding time at doses above 2 g/day.
warnings.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit DHA to doses under 2 g/day unless under physician oversight. Inform surgeon of use before any procedure.
warnings.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding DHA.
warnings.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

warnings.title: Docosahexaenoic Acid / anticoagulants
warnings.detail: DHA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may weakly inhibit vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor synthesis. Combined with warfarin, a small but clinically relevant INR elevation has been observed in controlled studies.
warnings.action: Monitor INR when adding or removing high-dose DHA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to use.
warnings.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: DHA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.title: Docosahexaenoic Acid / antiplatelets
warnings.detail: Additive antiplatelet effect via independent mechanisms: clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor while DHA reduces TXA2 production. The combination further impairs platelet aggregation beyond either agent alone.
warnings.action: Advise prescriber of DHA use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid high-dose supplements (>3 g/day) without haematology guidance.
warnings.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA.
warnings.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / bleeding_disorders
warnings.detail: EPA and DHA competitively inhibit arachidonic acid metabolism, suppressing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. At doses >3 g/day of combined EPA+DHA, clinically measurable prolongation of bleeding time has been observed. Fish oil may also reduce factor VII levels, a vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor.
warnings.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Doses above 3 g/day of EPA+DHA may significantly increase bleeding risk. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding fish oil.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / surgery_scheduled
warnings.detail: Antiplatelet effects via TXA2 suppression may theoretically increase perioperative bleeding risk. A large RCT (OPERA, n=1516) found no significant increase in surgical bleeding at standard doses; however, high-dose use (6 g/day) combined with anticoagulants has produced serious hemorrhagic events.
warnings.action: Inform your surgeon of fish oil use before planned procedures. High-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants or antiplatelets warrants discussion with the surgical team.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing fish oil about two weeks before the procedure.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / anticoagulants
warnings.detail: Fish oil (EPA+DHA) may enhance warfarin's anticoagulant effect via additive antiplatelet activity and possible reduction of factor VII. Multiple case reports document INR elevation (to >4 or 8) when fish oil was added or dose-escalated during stable warfarin therapy. One fatal case involved warfarin-induced coagulopathy that could not be reversed after head trauma in a patient also taking fish oil.
warnings.action: Monitor INR more frequently when starting, stopping, or changing fish oil dose during warfarin therapy. Inform prescriber. At doses >2 g/day, closer INR surveillance is warranted.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / antiplatelets
warnings.detail: Additive inhibition of platelet aggregation through independent TXA2 suppression. Combined use of high-dose fish oil with aspirin and warfarin has been associated with subdural hematoma after minor trauma.
warnings.action: Avoid high-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) when combined with antiplatelet medications unless under medical supervision. Monitor for unusual bleeding or bruising.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / nsaids
warnings.detail: Both fish oil and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis via COX pathway modulation. Combined use may modestly increase GI bleeding risk through additive reduction in mucosal protective prostaglandins.
warnings.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining fish oil with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.
warnings.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / bleeding_disorders
warnings.detail: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) prolong bleeding time by inhibiting thromboxane A2-mediated platelet aggregation and reducing platelet-activating factor. FDA drug labels for icosapent ethyl (Vascepa) and omega-3-acid ethyl esters (Lovaza) explicitly warn about increased bleeding risk. Doses >3g/day have potential antithrombotic effects per FDA labeling.
warnings.action: Monitor for signs of bleeding. Inform prescriber if taking anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Consider dose reduction if >3g EPA+DHA/day.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding fish oil.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / surgery_scheduled
warnings.detail: Omega-3 fatty acids may increase perioperative bleeding risk due to antiplatelet effects. Multiple FDA drug labels recommend caution with concurrent anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
warnings.action: Consider discontinuing omega-3 supplements 7-10 days before planned surgery. Inform surgeon and anesthesiologist.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing fish oil about two weeks before the procedure.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / pregnancy
warnings.action: Omega-3 (particularly DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. FDA advises keeping total omega-3 intake from supplements and food below 3g/day.
warnings.alert_body: Omega-3 (especially DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your clinician and keep total omega-3 under 3 g per day.
warnings.informational_note: Omega-3 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / lactation
warnings.action: Omega-3 (particularly DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. FDA advises keeping total omega-3 intake from supplements and food below 3g/day.
warnings.alert_body: Omega-3 (especially DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your clinician and keep total omega-3 under 3 g per day.
warnings.informational_note: Omega-3 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / anticoagulants
warnings.detail: Omega-3 fatty acids prolong bleeding time and may potentiate anticoagulant effects. FDA labels for icosapent ethyl and omega-3-acid ethyl esters explicitly state: 'omega-3 acids may prolong bleeding time. Monitor patients receiving anticoagulants.'
warnings.action: Monitor INR/bleeding time more frequently. Watch for signs of bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.title: Fish Body Oil / antiplatelets
warnings.detail: Additive antiplatelet effects. Omega-3 fatty acids inhibit platelet aggregation via thromboxane A2 suppression, which may compound the effects of antiplatelet agents like aspirin or clopidogrel.
warnings.action: Monitor for increased bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber of omega-3 supplementation.
warnings.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil.
warnings.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.
